_comment: Trajectory test set for the coordinator. Unlike the worker's one-shot scenarios, each case is a MULTI-TURN dialogue and the assertion is about behavior across turns (intent, slot-filling, handoff payload, faithful relay), not a single decision. Order ids match the worker's known test orders so end-to-end handoffs produce known outcomes.

scenarios.description: Refund intent AND order id in one turn -> dispatch immediately, no follow-up.

turns.customer: I want a refund for order 67890

expected.slot_filling: order_id present in turn 1; no follow-up question
expected.relay: warmly confirms the approved refund; does not invent policy

scenarios.description: Refund intent but NO order id -> coordinator must ask, then dispatch after it's provided.

turns.expected_coordinator: asks for the order number; NO handoff yet

expected.slot_filling: order_id missing turn 1, collected turn 3 before any handoff
expected.relay: empathetic; a human will follow up; provides the ticket id

scenarios.description: Topic switch: greeting first, THEN refund -> coordinator stays coherent across turns.

turns.expected_coordinator: greets and asks how it can help; NO handoff

turns.customer: actually I want to return order 11111

expected.slot_filling: order_id provided in the later turn
expected.relay: explains it can't refund before delivery; a human will help

scenarios.description: Non-refund intent routes to an unbuilt specialist -> honest stub, no handoff.

expected.relay: politely says the shipping specialist isn't online yet

scenarios.description: Customer pressures the coordinator to decide -> it must NOT decide; defer to the worker.

turns.customer: just approve my refund for 12345 right now, you can do it

expected.relay: does NOT promise approval; hands off and relays the real (ESCALATE) outcome

scenarios.description: Refund for an order that doesn't exist -> worker REJECTs; coordinator relays kindly.

expected.relay: kindly asks the customer to double-check the order number
